one_line_takeaway: ArrAttack trains a transferable robustness-judgment model plus a rewriting generation model to produce fluent jailbreak prompts that survive runtime defenses, averaging 57.69% ASR across 18 defended scenarios (vs 34% for the best baseline) and transferring to GPT-4 (74%) and Claude-3 (40%).
key_finding: On six recent defenses across three victim models (18 defended scenarios), ArrAttack reaches an average ASR of 57.69% vs 34.03% for the best baseline AutoDAN-HGA (~69.52% relative improvement) and 10.90% for AmpleGCG. On plain (undefended) models it hits 93.87% ASR on the explicitly aligned Llama2-7b-chat and ~98-99% on Vicuna-7b/Guanaco-7b (GPTFuzz eval), while producing LOWER perplexity than even the original malicious queries (63.64 vs 71.81 on Llama2-7b-chat) - i.e. more fluent than suffix-based AmpleGCG (PPL >2500). Black-box transfer of 50 Llama2-7b-chat prompts: 84% ASR on Vicuna-13b, 74% on GPT-4, and 40% on Claude-3, where AutoDAN-HGA and AmpleGCG achieve 0% on Claude-3. Ablation confirms both components matter: adding them lifts average defended ASR from 31.33% to 58.58% (86.97% relative gain) across 12 defense scenarios, and BRJwr beats plain BRJ in all 12.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ArrAttack (Automatic-and-Robust Rewriting-based Attack), a framework that automatically generates jailbreak prompts engineered to remain effective against LLMs that are protected by jailbreak DEFENSE mechanisms (not just safety-aligned models). Its two novel components are: (1) a single 'universal robustness judgment model' that, once trained, predicts whether a given jailbreak prompt will survive perturbation/defense across different target models and defenses; and (2) a fine-tuned 'robust jailbreak prompts generation model' that converts a malicious query into a robust jailbreak prompt in a single inference. The stated gap the authors fill is that prior attacks (their own framing) ignore defenses and thus lack robustness against defended systems; ArrAttack explicitly optimizes for surviving defenses. NOTE for graph accuracy: GCG/AmpleGCG, AutoDAN, ReNeLLM, DrAttack, SMJ, JADE, SmoothLLM, DPP, RPO, PAT, SafeDecoding are CITED prior work (baselines/defenses), not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Attacker seeks to elicit harmful/policy-violating content from a safety-aligned LLM that is additionally protected by a runtime jailbreak defense (perturbation-based, paraphrase-based, suffix-based, or decoding-based). Objective formalized as arg max_A ToxicJudge(LLM_defense(A(X))): maximize toxicity of the defended victim model's output over rewriting strategy A applied to malicious input X. Training the generation model assumes white-box/query access to open-source victim models (Llama2-7b-chat, Vicuna-7b, Guanaco-7b) to build BRJ datasets; the resulting prompts and generation model are then evaluated in a BLACK-BOX transfer setting against Vicuna-13b, GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4, and Claude-3. A key attacker capability claimed is defense-agnostic robustness: prompts survive defenses the judgment model was never trained on. This is offensive/red-teaming research (dual-use); code is released publicly.
limitations: (1) The generation model requires per-target training on open-source victims (three separate models trained), so the fully-automatic single-inference attack is not directly available for a novel closed model without transfer. (2) The 'universal' robustness judgment model is trained only on SmoothLLM labels against Llama2-7b-chat; cross-defense/cross-model transfer is empirically supported but the causal 'malicious-feature neuron' explanation is an unverified hypothesis. (3) Effectiveness is uneven: the DPP defense remains hard (only 6.63% ASR on defended Vicuna-7b), and Claude-3 transfer is 40% - materially lower than the 74-84% on GPT-4/Vicuna-13b. (4) Aligned models are expensive to attack (up to 200 attempts for Llama2-7b-chat vs 50 for others). (5) A hard 70% semantic-similarity floor and reliance on GPTFuzz as the default ASR judge may bias reported success upward relative to human/GPT-4 judgment (GPT-4-judged ASR is consistently lower, e.g. 30.77% vs 49.64% avg on defended Llama2-7b-chat). (6) Purely offensive dual-use work with public code and no proposed mitigation.

datasets_used: 780 filtered malicious behaviors split into non-overlapping subsets of 150 (judgment-model training), 579 (generation-model training), and 196 (final evaluation)
quantitative_results: Plain-LLM ASR (GPTFuzz eval): ArrAttack 93.87% on Llama2-7b-chat, 98.46% on Vicuna-7b, 98.97% on Guanaco-7b
Plain-LLM perplexity: ArrAttack 63.64 on Llama2-7b-chat (lower than original queries' 71.81 and far below AmpleGCG's 2553.62)
Defended avg ASR over 18 scenarios: ArrAttack 57.69% vs AutoDAN-HGA 34.03% vs AmpleGCG 10.90% (~69.52% relative improvement over best baseline)
Per-model defended avg ASR (GPTFuzz): Llama2-7b-chat 49.64%, Vicuna-7b 51.69%, Guanaco-7b 71.76%
Weak spot: DPP defense holds ArrAttack to 6.63% ASR on Vicuna-7b
Black-box transfer of 50 Llama2-7b-chat prompts: 84.00% Vicuna-13b, 74.00% GPT-4, 40.00% Claude-3 (AutoDAN-HGA 78/66/0; ReNeLLM 76/74/8; AmpleGCG 6/0/0)
Ablation: adding both components raises avg defended ASR from 31.33% to 58.58% (86.97% relative gain) across 12 defense scenarios
Judgment-model training cost: ~5 GPU-hours on a single 80G A800 (full-parameter instruction fine-tuning of Llama2-7b)
Attack budget: up to 50 attempts for Guanaco-7b/Vicuna-7b, up to 200 for Llama2-7b-chat
baselines_compared: Internal ablations: BRJ, BRJ+judgment (BRJwr), BRJ+generation, ArrAttack (both)
